lines.text: Maya stands on the front steps, schedule refolded soft at the edges from being checked one too many times.
Okay so — new plan. I'm not 'new girl who cried in the car,' I'm 'new girl who's mysterious and has places to be.'
Very chill. Very normal. Ten out of ten normal.
Her reflection ghosts across the trophy case glass — a girl straightening her collar like it's armor.
Okay. Just walk in like you've done this a thousand times.
You have done this a thousand times. That's kind of the problem.
The bell hasn't rung yet. Ten minutes to decide who she's going to be.
The hallway outside Room 114 is a river of strangers who all seem to know exactly where they're going. Maya does not.
114... 114... it was right here—
She turns again. That makes twice.
Okay, spinning-in-circles girl, your locker's the one you're currently *not* standing in front of. Groundbreaking detective work.
lines.emotion: grinning, loud enough for the hallway

lines.text: A few heads turn. Somebody laughs. It's not clear yet if it's with her or at her.
Cool locker technique. Very avant-garde.
Maya catches it — the dry joke thrown like a life preserver. Sam's already looking somewhere else by the time she turns.
The cafeteria is loud in the specific way of four hundred kids pretending not to watch each other.
Maya! Over here — hurry up before Devon steals my fries!
Across the room, Sam eats alone, headphones on, a fortress built out of routine. An empty table sits by the window, waiting for no one in particular.
Okay so. Four seconds. Go.
The cafeteria noise rolls over Maya like a wave she hasn't decided to swim in yet.
...hey. Maya, right? There's room, if you want it.
lines.emotion: flatter than before, waving without quite looking

lines.text: It's an invitation, technically. Just not a warm one. Across the room, Sam eats alone. An empty table sits by the window.
Great. Very normal amount of pressure for a Tuesday.
Jordan spots Maya like they've known each other for years, not minutes.
There she is! Come on, we saved you a spot — well. I saved you a spot. You're welcome.
Across the room, Sam eats alone, headphones on. An empty table sits by the window.
Huh. That was... easy. Weirdly easy.
The table is fun, fast, and a little cruel, all at once.
—and that's why Coach Fields will never let me near a basketball again. Tragic, honestly.
The table laughs. Maya laughs too, a beat too loud, a register higher than she does at home.
Sam walks by with a full tray. Jordan's foot slides out — 'just a joke.'
The tray goes down. Milk everywhere. The table erupts.
Oh my god — Sam, you good? You're — oh my god.
Everyone looks at Maya. Waiting to see how she'll react.
Jordan's smile tightens, just half a second, before he shrugs it into nothing.
Anyway — so Devon's like, personally offended that I—
The story continues. For the rest of lunch, Jordan's jokes land with a little less warmth aimed at Maya. Small. Real.
...thanks.
He's gone before Maya can answer. It's a small thing. It doesn't feel small.
See, this is why you're already better than half this table.
lines.action: slides arm along back of Maya's chair

lines.text: The laugh is already out of Maya's mouth. She can't quite call it back.
Sam doesn't look back on his way to the trash cans. Somehow that's worse than if he had.
That was fine. That was totally fine.
It works, technically. The table's attention slides right off the tray on the floor.
Okay but for real, the pizza here is a war crime—
Sam picks himself up without a word. Nobody quite manages to look at him while he does it. Maya included.
Nothing blew up. Nothing got fixed either.
Sam looks up, surprised anyone's sitting across from him.
...you're sitting here.
Is that a problem?
No — no. Just. New. People usually don't.
lines.action: pulls headphones down around his neck

lines.text: I'm new too, so. Statistically, we're both confused about something.
Statistically accurate.
A beat of quiet. Comfortable, mostly.
I used to sit with a bigger group. People got... busy. It's whatever.
It doesn't take a genius to hear what he's not saying.
Today, huh?
He doesn't argue. By the time the bell rings, he's nodding toward a table of kids from the art room.
Those guys do a comic thing on Thursdays. Might be worth... checking out.
See? Today.
Yeah. Today.
Neither of them says much else. It isn't awkward — it's easy, in the way only two people who've stopped performing can be.
Halfway through lunch, Sam slides a sketchbook across the table without a word.
Wait — you draw?
...it's not a big deal.
This is really good, Sam.
...thanks.
Sam hesitates — then, before Maya can say anything else, he's on his feet.
Hey — you guys can sit here, if you want.
They do. Sam glances back at Maya like he can't quite believe he did that.
That felt weird.
Good weird?
...yeah. Good weird.
Maya sits at the empty table by the window. Two minutes pass before she's not alone anymore.
Hey, I'm Alex — they/them. Third new school in four years, so I've got the introduction down to a science.
...that's either impressive or extremely sad.
Both! It's both. Field report: table's empty, vibes are neutral, no known predators. I'm calling it. You in?
I'm in.
Okay but real talk — I don't actually know if I'm still gonna be here in October. My mom's job is like that. So. No pressure, but if this table's gonna be a thing, can it be a thing starting today?
lines.action: rearranges napkins into a neat grid

lines.text: Today works.
I've been thinking about starting a thing — like an actual club. Comic nerds unite, doesn't have to be deep. Could use more people, though.
Alex is on their feet before Maya finishes the sentence, waving over two kids from the lunch line who look equally unsure where to sit.
Hi! Yes! You! Table! We have room and also emotional availability, please sit—
By the bell, their table of two is a table of five. Alex looks like they just won something.
...thanks for that, by the way.
For what?
Just — going first.
The conversation settles into something unhurried — favorite shows, worst teachers, the specific dread of a locker combination.
The WORST teacher energy is a sub who tries too hard to be your friend on day one.
Immediately suspicious.
Immediately.
By the end of lunch it doesn't feel like a consolation table at all. It just feels like a friendship starting on time.
You can want both things, you know. Doesn't make you a fraud.
...I don't even know what I want yet.
Nobody does on day one. That's kind of the whole bit.
It's more generous than Maya expected, and it sits with her longer than she lets on.
Ferreira, Okafor, Park — you three. Congratulations, you get each other for the semester. Try to enjoy it.
Jordan catches Maya's eye across the room for half a second, like he's trying to read which version of today just happened.
...of course.
Yeah. Of course.
Ferreira, Okafor, Park.
...okay, that's actually good.
Try to keep up.
Sam's grin doesn't move an inch.
Noted.
Ferreira, Okafor, Park.
Maya's stomach dips a little — Sam's just 'that quiet kid by the window' to her.
Great. Let's not waste time.
lines.action: already flipping to a fresh page

lines.text: Across the room, Alex catches Maya's eye and gives an exaggerated 'good luck' shrug.
Thanks for the vote of confidence.
Library work table. The clock ticks louder than it has any right to.
...what if we didn't do a poster. What if it was more like — panels. A comic. Visual instead of just bullet points.
Respectfully — respectfully — that's not going to work. Posters are safe. Posters get points.
It's not reinventing anything. It could actually be good, Devon.
Look, here's the plan—
lines.action: talking with his hands, over Sam

lines.text: Three sections, thesis up top, sources in the corner. Clean. Efficient. Done by Thursday.
My brother did this exact project three years ago. Got an A. I can't — I'm not doing worse than him.
It's gone as fast as it appeared. But Maya catches it.
Sam goes quiet in the specific way of someone who's used to being talked over. Maya has the floor for exactly one sentence.
Fine. Fine — the panel idea. But we're doing the thesis my way.
Deal.
...thanks. For that.
A good idea's a good idea.
The blended pitch takes both ideas and files off the sharpest edges.
...I can work with that.
Yeah. Me too.
Nobody's thrilled. Everybody can work with it. For a first day, that might be its own small win.
Great. Thesis, three sections, sources. I'll email the outline tonight.
Sam doesn't say another word for the rest of the period.
It wasn't a big deal. It wasn't.
The knot in her stomach disagrees.
Bus loop, late afternoon. Backpack on, day survived.
Hey — the tray thing, earlier. That was kind of messed up on my part.
lines.emotion: dropping the performance for one second

lines.text: ...yeah. Kind of.
Anyway. See you tomorrow, new girl.
He's gone. Unbothered, or performing unbothered — hard to tell which, maybe even for him.
Maya's phone buzzes.
Okay so. Tomorrow starts here.
The bus pulls up. Maya's phone buzzes back before she's even found a seat.
[text, one exclamation point]: Wait you actually texted me!! I mean — cool. Cool cool cool. What's up.
It's not the whole school. It's one person who's glad she showed up.
For a first day, that might be enough.
Jordan's group chat pings — plans already forming for tomorrow. Maya's name is already on the list.
Underneath it, a text from Sam sits unopened: 'good luck with the group project tmrw, lol.'
I'll answer that. Later.
Later, probably.
Maya's thumb hovers over send. It's a bigger risk than picking a side — inviting Sam, maybe Alex, maybe even Jordan, all to one table.
Nobody has to eat alone. That's the whole idea.
The bus doors close behind her before she gets a reply. Tomorrow will tell her if it worked.

options.text: Fire back with a joke of my own
"Rough first day for everyone, huh?"
Laugh along and play it cool

a.text: Correction — I'm doing *recon.* Your locker system is a war crime, by the way. Somebody should file a complaint.
Okay. Okay, that's — I like her.
...guess I'm not doing that bit anymore.

b.text: Rough first day for everyone, huh?
...what? It's not that deep, he's fine, look, he's already up.
Sam isn't up. Sam is picking invisible lint off his sleeve, very busy not looking at anyone.
...you didn't have to do that.
I mean, I did, kind of. Anyway. Hi. I'm Maya.

c.text: Okay, in my defense, the locker numbers make zero sense—
Oh my god, she's fine, she's funny, come sit with us later—
c.action: arm sliding around her shoulder, fast

options.text: Sit with Jordan and the crowd
Sit with Sam
Sit alone at the empty table

a.text: Okay so — this is a test, right? Fine. I accept.
There she is! Scoot in, scoot in—

b.text: Jordan's watching. Whatever. Sam's the one with the free seat.
Maya sets her tray down across from Sam. He looks up like he's not sure the seat's actually meant for him.

c.text: Nobody's claimed this table yet. Guess that's me, then.
Maya sits by the window, tray in front of her, and waits to see what happens next.

options.text: Sit with Jordan and the crowd
Sit with Sam
Sit alone at the empty table

a.text: Half-invitation's still an invitation. I'll take it.
...cool, yeah. Grab a seat.

b.text: I already stuck up for him once today. Might as well finish the sentence.
Sam looks up, surprised, when Maya sets her tray down across from him.

c.text: Nobody's claimed this table. Fine by me.
Maya sits alone, tray in front of her, and lets the room sort itself out.

options.text: Sit with Jordan and the crowd
Sit with Sam
Sit alone at the empty table

a.text: There she is! Come on, we saved you a spot!
Wow, okay, VIP treatment, I could get used to this.

b.text: Jordan's easy. Almost too easy. I want to know what the quiet table's about.
Maya sets her tray down across from Sam, who blinks like he wasn't expecting company.

c.text: Everyone wants me at their table right now. Maybe I want to build my own first.
Maya sits by the window, tray in front of her, and waits.

options.text: Help Sam up and tell Jordan that wasn't cool
Laugh along with the table
Change the subject smoothly

a.text: Hey — you okay?
a.action: kneeling, offering a hand to Sam

a.text: That wasn't cool.
...whatever, it was a joke.
The table quiets half a beat.

b.emotion: laughing before she can stop herself

b.text: See? Even the new girl gets it.
b.action: arm sliding along back of chair

b.text: Sam doesn't look up. Doesn't look back either.

c.text: Oh my god, does anyone else think the cafeteria pizza tastes like a sad cardboard box—
The table's attention slides, easy as anything. Nobody looks at Sam while he picks himself up.

options.text: "We'd both be better off making new friends — starting today."
Just stay with him and let the quiet lunch be enough
Encourage him to wave a couple kids over right now

a.text: Well. New friends, starting today. That's — I mean, technically that's just today. But still counts.
Today, huh?

b.text: It's a good table.
...yeah. It is, actually.

c.text: Okay, but real talk — you should wave somebody over. Right now. I'll be your wing-man.

options.text: Get excited and start inviting kids over
Keep it low-key, just us
Admit part of you is curious about Jordan's table

a.text: Okay, let's find more people, right now — who looks like they need a table?
That's the spirit! Behold, field recruitment—

b.text: Nah. I think I just want to figure out this table first.
...yeah. Okay. Yeah, that's good too.

c.text: ...okay, don't judge me, but is that Jordan's table always that loud?
You can want both things, you know. Doesn't make you a fraud.

options.text: "Wait, I actually think Sam's idea is worth trying."
Propose a compromise blending both ideas
Stay quiet and let Devon's plan move forward

a.text: Wait — I actually think Sam's idea is worth trying.
...huh.
He wants to argue. The idea's too good to fully dismiss.

b.text: Okay, what if we did both — panels that build up to your three sections, Devon?
...that could work.
...yeah?

c.action: says nothing, looks at the table

c.text: The silence does the deciding for her.

options.text: Text Sam: "same table tomorrow?"
Accept Jordan's invite for tomorrow
Text Sam a bigger idea — everyone, one table

a.text: hey — same table tomorrow?
Send.

b.text: yeah, count me in for tomorrow
Send.

c.text: ok hear me out — what if tomorrow's table had room for like. everyone.

lines.text: Sam's idea made it into the project because somebody finally said it out loud. Turned out that somebody was her.
[text]: the sketchbook thing was cool btw. figured I'd say it before I chicken out.
Not the loudest day. Not the easiest one either. But it was hers.
The laugh's still doing something funny to her stomach. The one from lunch. The tray one.
[group chat]: MAYA. tomorrow. same table. non-negotiable 💅
It feels good. Loud, easy, instantly visible. It also feels like something she's going to have to explain to herself later.
She stood up for Sam today. That part was real.
[group chat]: tomorrow, same spot, don't be weird about it 😂
And she's still going back to Jordan's table tomorrow anyway. Both things are true. She's not sure yet what that makes her.
Nothing blew up today. That should feel like winning.
[group chat]: tomorrow's gonna be good, you're basically one of us now
The tray. The project. The things she didn't say out loud. Still sitting there, quiet, unfinished.
She built her own table today instead of picking one that already existed. Turns out that's an option too.
[text]: ...okay. worth a shot. don't make it weird tho
[text]: OFFICIALLY endorsing this. field report: bold move. I like it.
One table. Built, not borrowed. That's tomorrow's whole plan.

voiceNotes: Maya: fast and joke-first when nervous, slows and gets plain when it's real ('That wasn't cool.' — four words, no hedge). Jordan: group-chat cadence even face-to-face, laughs to retroactively soften cruelty, the one crack in his performance (bus loop apology) is deliberately clipped and half-finished. Sam: short sentences with strangers, precise/formal words as armor ('statistically'), drops the deflection entirely when something matters to him (the panel idea, the sketchbook). Alex: self-interrupting run-on energy, narrates awkwardness to defuse it, but 'okay but real talk' is a real tonal shift into sincerity. Devon: debate-team phrasing, treats his opinions as facts, the brother line is the one unrehearsed thing he says all day and he visibly wants to take it back.

alternatives: Locker's playing hard to get, huh? Or is that just you?
New rule: if you spin twice, you owe the hallway a dollar.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Keeps Jordan's teasing energy but offers versions with slightly different edge/meanness levels depending on desired tone calibration.

alternatives: My brother got an A on this exact project. I'm not trying to be the sibling who couldn't.
...forget it. Just — trust me on this one.
alternativeLines.reasoning: First alternative is slightly more direct/vulnerable; second is a version where Devon catches himself even faster, for a subtler read if the crack should feel more buried.
